title: Κύων κρέας φέρουσα
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 185 (= Perry 133). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.l: A dog, meat holding, a river was-crossing:
sentences.i: A dog was crossing a river with a piece of meat,
sentences.n: κύων is feminine here (ἔχουσα) — a she-dog. The imperfect διέβαινε holds her mid-crossing while the disaster assembles.

sentences.t: θεασαμένη δὲ τὴν ἑαυτῆς σκιὰν κατὰ τοῦ ὕδατος, ὑπέλαβεν ἑτέραν κύνα εἶναι μεῖζον κρέας ἔχουσαν.
sentences.tr: theasamenē de tēn heautēs skian kata tou hydatos, hypelaben heteran kyna einai meizon kreas echousan.
sentences.l: and having-caught-sight-of the of-herself shadow down-on the water, she-supposed another dog to-be, a bigger piece-of-meat holding.
sentences.i: and catching sight of her own reflection in the water, she took it for another dog holding a bigger piece of meat.
sentences.n: σκιά is 'shadow' — Greek uses the same word for a reflection. ὑπέλαβεν … εἶναι: 'supposed (it) to be' — the accusative + infinitive of thinking, with the participle ἔχουσαν agreeing with the imagined dog.

sentences.t: Διόπερ ἀφεῖσα τὸ ἴδιον ὥρμησεν ὡς τὸ ἐκείνης ἀφαιρησομένη.
sentences.tr: Dioper apheisa to idion hōrmēsen hōs to ekeinēs aphairēsomenē.
sentences.l: Wherefore, having-let-go the own, she-lunged as the of-that-one about-to-seize.
sentences.i: So she dropped her own and lunged to seize the other dog's.
sentences.n: ὡς + future participle (ἀφαιρησομένη) expresses purpose: 'as one intending to take.' τὸ ἴδιον / τὸ ἐκείνης — 'her own' versus 'that one's': the whole delusion in two article-phrases.

sentences.t: Συνέβη δὲ αὐτῇ ἀμφοτέρων στερηθῆναι, τοῦ μὲν μὴ ἐφικομένῃ, διότι οὐδὲ ἦν, τοῦ δέ, ὅτι ὑπὸ τοῦ ποταμοῦ παρεσύρη.
sentences.tr: Synebē de autēi amphoterōn sterēthēnai, tou men mē ephikomenēi, dioti oude ēn, tou de, hoti hypo tou potamou paresyrē.
sentences.l: And it-happened to-her of-both to-be-deprived: the one not reaching, because it-was not even; the other, because by the river it-was-swept-away.
sentences.i: The result was that she lost both: the one she could not get, because it never existed; the other, because the river swept it away.
sentences.n: συνέβη + dative + infinitive again ('it befell her to…'), and στερηθῆναι with the genitive — the exact construction that ended the Shepherd Who Cried Wolf. A tidy μέν … δέ splits the two losses.

sentences.t: Πρὸς ἄνδρα πλεονέκτην ὁ λόγος εὔκαιρος.
sentences.tr: Pros andra pleonektēn ho logos eukairos.
sentences.l: Against a man grasping the story well-timed.
sentences.i: The story suits a greedy man.
sentences.n: πλεονέκτης — literally 'one who has (or wants) more', the Greek vice-word for overreaching greed. The εὔκαιρος moral formula again, one fable after you first met it.
